data.title: Alpine Income Property Trust signals 2026 investment volume of $170M-$200M while raising FFO outlook to $2.09-$2.13
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased to report a strong first quarter in 2026, building on a record level of investment activity we achieved in 2025," said President, CEO & Director John Albright. "We continue to execute our investment strategy by seeking to assemble a high-quality portfolio of single-tenant net lease properties leased to investment-grade rated tenants in addition to originating commercial loans with attractive risk-adjusted returns..."

  • "During the quarter, we acquired a retail property in downtown Aspen, Colorado, for $10 million," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "This acquisition was structured as a 50-year absolute triple-net master lease and initial cap rate of 8.5% with 1.25% annual rent escalators." He also said the company "continue[s] to selectively prune" the portfolio, "selling 3 non-investment-grade-rated lease properties for $5.8 million."

  • "As a result of our combined first quarter property transactions, our property portfolio consists of 125 properties...with a 99.5% occupancy and a WALT of 9.3 years," said President, CEO & Director Albright, adding that "50% of our ABR is generated from investment-grade rated tenants."

  • "During the quarter, we originated a $32 million first mortgage loan, of which $8.6 million was funded at close," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "The loan carries a 24-month term with an initial interest rate of 13% inclusive of a 1.5% paid-in-kind interest, stepping down to an 11.5% current pay rate upon the borrower meeting certain conditions."

  • "We closed and funded the $31.8 million Phase 2 of our first mortgage loan investment secured by a luxury residential development located in Austin, Texas metropolitan area," said President, CEO & Director Albright. "Accordingly, net of the A-1 participation, our combined investment in Phase 1 and Phase 2 of this loan was $40 million at quarter end." He said the commercial loan portfolio totaled "$160.4 million" and that the loan mix reached "our targeted level of approximately 20% of our total undepreciated asset value."

  • "For the quarter, total revenue was $18.4 million, including lease income of $12.6 million and interest income from commercial loan investments of $5.8 million," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Philip Mays. "FFO and AFFO for the quarter were both $0.53 per diluted share."

Outlook

  • "We are raising our 2026 outlook for investment volume by $100 million," said President, CEO & Director Albright.

  • "For the full year 2026, we are increasing our FFO outlook to a new range of $2.09 to $2.13 per diluted share and our AFFO outlook to a new range of $2.11 to $2.15 per diluted share," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "Further...we are increasing our investment activity by $100 million to a new range of $170 million to $200 million." (In the prior quarter’s call, the company’s initial 2026 investment volume assumption was "$70 million to $100 million".)

  • "The guidance doesn't assume any incentive fee," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays.

Financial Results

  • "During the first quarter, we amended and restated our unsecured credit facility," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "Our new facility includes a $250 million revolver due February 2030...a $100 million term loan maturing in 2029 and a $100 million term loan maturing in 2031." He added, "with the closing of this facility, we now have no debt maturing for almost 3 years."

  • "Under our common ATM, we issued approximately 1.7 million shares...for net proceeds of $31.6 million," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "And under our preferred ATM...for net proceeds of $4.6 million." He said the quarter ended with "net debt to pro forma adjusted EBITDA of 6.6x and approximately $90 million of liquidity."

  • "The Board increased our quarterly common dividend by 5.3%...to $0.30 per share beginning this quarter," said Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays. "This new quarterly common dividend rate represents just a 57% AFFO payout ratio for the quarter."

Q&A

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank: "can you just talk a little bit about...what's in the pipeline"; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "we have a fair amount of activity in the pipeline...trying to bring in some additional investment-grade credits" and that "as we have some lower-yielding loans burn off...That will be a nice recycle into higher-yielding and high-quality loans."

  • Jay Kornreich, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked if being near the 20% loan target implies "a shift in strategy" toward net lease; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "we do have a larger amount in the pipeline of traditional net lease investments," while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays added, "we are near that 20% cap kind of an 80-20 blend."

  • Matthew Erdner, JonesTrading: asked about "loan to own" outcomes and lease rollover; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "most likely, none of these will turn into ownership positions" and on 2027-2028 rollover risk said, "feel very strong that these are going to be renewal candidates."

  • R.J. Milligan, Raymond James: asked whether any loans are on a watch list and about PIK collectability; Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays said, "We don't really have any credit concerns about any of the loans," and President, CEO & Director Albright added, "out of our loan book, there are no concerns right now."

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about cap-rate expectations; President, CEO & Director Albright said, "you're going to be closer to 7.5% this coming quarter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, concentrating on allocation limits (the 20% loan target), visibility into loan credit/PIK repayment, lease rollover/mark-to-market constraints, and acquisition pricing.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, leaning on confidence language around pipeline and credit: "We feel very confident" (President, CEO & Director Albright) and "We don't really have any credit concerns" (Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the tone shifted toward higher confidence on 2026 activity and capital deployment, supported by raised guidance and larger stated investment plans.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language turned more expansionary: Q1 2026 included "increasing" both earnings outlook ranges and lifting investment volume to "$170 million to $200 million," compared with Q4 2025’s initial assumptions of "$70 million to $100 million" investment volume and "$30 million to $60 million" disposition volume.

  • Strategic emphasis remained consistent on the property-plus-loans model, but Q1 2026 framed the loan portfolio as having reached the targeted scale: "now grown to our targeted level of approximately 20%."

  • Analysts continued probing loan allocation and credit visibility in both quarters; Q1 2026 also placed more emphasis on cap-rate trajectory and refinancing/ATM activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Lease rollover upside appears constrained by option structures: "They have options. So unfortunately, it's going to be a set bump based on the renewal options," said President, CEO & Director Albright.

  • Funding cadence and earnings variability risk from loan payoffs was acknowledged: President, CEO & Director Albright said that as loans pay off, "there's something else in the pipeline" and described "lower-yielding loans" rolling off with planned reinvestment.

  • Loan underwriting conservatism and reserve mechanics were highlighted as mitigants: "we basically want to really have these loans structured pretty tightly," said President, CEO & Director Albright, while Senior VP, CFO & Treasurer Mays described "pretty healthy reserves upfront" tied to items like taxes and interest.

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a quarter of continued portfolio recycling and loan growth, highlighted by a $10 million Aspen acquisition, higher-yielding loan originations, and an expanded funding base via ATMs and a recast credit facility. The company raised full-year 2026 guidance ranges for both FFO and AFFO per diluted share and increased its targeted 2026 investment activity to $170 million-$200 million, while maintaining that the loan book has reached its intended ~20% scale and that the pipeline supports reinvestment as repayments occur.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hims rises as J.P. Morgan initiates at Overweight on rich catalyst path
data.description:
Shares of Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) gained on Friday after J.P. Morgan launched its coverage with an Overweight recommendation and a Dec. 2026 price target of $35, citing a compelling catalyst path for the telehealth platform.

Analyst Cory Carpenter argued that Hims’ (HIMS) recent partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO) could be a “turning point” for the company after GLP-1 sales, which represented more than 35% of its business, faced uncertainty as the shortage for the popular drug class ended.

“We think the recent Novo partnership could mark a turning point, removing a significant legal overhang and positioning HIMS as a platform that offers branded, generic, and compounded products,” Carpenter added.

The analyst argued that the FDA’s recent move to loosen restrictions on 12 peptides, enabling their mass compounding, could expand the company’s total addressable market and establish a potentially meaningful new specialty for HIMS, that could generate upside in 2027.

“The combination of a more stable GLP-1 business, peptide legalization, revenue reacceleration in 2H, and the potential for more branded partnerships over time in our view creates a compelling catalyst path against a skeptical investor base,” Carpenter wrote.

data.title: Rithm Property Trust outlines Genesis production of $6B-$7B and EBITDA of $150M-$200M while looking to redeploy cash
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rithm Property Trust (RPT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Michael Nierenberg framed Q1 as “a pretty uneventful quarter” while emphasizing patience on deployment: “With asset manager valuations under pressure, downward pressure on equity valuations in the public markets, we're going to continue to remain patient and work towards creating value for shareholders.”

  • Nierenberg highlighted balance sheet flexibility and optionality, stating, “Today, the company has just a little under $100 million of cash and liquidity. The balance sheet is extremely clean. There's no problem loans,” and added, “While we continue to wait for the opportunity to transform the company, we'll continue to pay the dividend.”

  • Nierenberg described the current footprint and pipeline: “Today, the pipeline is, give or take, about $2 billion,” alongside “The equity is a little bit under $300 million. It's about $287 million,” and “The commercial real estate portfolio... is $236 million.”

  • CFO Nicola Santoro addressed expense volatility, saying, “That was a onetime event in the quarter. It had to do with us looking at various capital options.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit forward EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A; discussion centered on timing and targets for redeploying liquidity and potentially scaling the vehicle.

  • Nierenberg indicated near-term investment timing, telling an analyst, “we're looking at a large portfolio now of multifamily assets that will be coming at some point in May,” and said the company is also “seeing some opportunities on the debt side... that I think we'll be able to deploy capital at higher yields.”

  • On capital actions, Nierenberg said, “at some point, we may consider even buying back a little bit of stock here,” while also pointing to M&A as a path to scale: “we'll be looking at different opportunities in the M&A world.”

Financial Results

  • Nierenberg reported “GAAP income, negative $3.2 million or $0.42 per diluted share” and “Earnings available for distribution, negative $300,000 or $0.04 per diluted share.”

  • Nierenberg reiterated shareholder distributions: “Dividend paid in the quarter, $0.36 per diluted share, which correlates to about a 10.8% dividend yield based on where the equity is trading today.”

  • Nierenberg cited balance sheet and valuation markers: “Book value, $236.2 million or $30.83,” and “cash and liquidity a little under $100 million.” He also said the company “sold a few CRE floaters in the quarter to create a little liquidity, looking for better opportunities.”

Q&A

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked if the quarter’s CMBS reduction meant lower leverage for longer; Title Nierenberg responded the sales were “to create more capital for what I would call opportunistic investing,” adding “at some point, that capital will get redeployed... multifamily or even buying back some equity here.”

  • Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked if RPT would keep harvesting CMBS if the backdrop stays weak; Title Nierenberg said “we're just looking for better opportunities to actually create more earnings,” while noting RMBS/CMBS liquidity and that “things are actually okay” despite geopolitical headlines.

  • Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about the jump in professional fees; Title Santoro answered, “That was a onetime event in the quarter. It had to do with us looking at various capital options.”

  • Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: asked about Paramount’s earnings contribution; Title Santoro said, “Paramount for the quarter was essentially flat,” and later added, “No, it will ramp up as the investment continues to accrete.”

  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point: asked about Genesis loan flow and unsecured debt; Title Nierenberg outlined Genesis expectations: “this year, I think we're going to do something between $6 billion and $7 billion of production, and the company should make between $150 million and $200 million of EBITDA,” and described exploring securitization economics “north of 20% or 15% to 20%” for certain assets.

  • Henry Coffey, Wedbush: pressed on what delays deployment; Title Nierenberg said “This vehicle... is extremely small” and emphasized scaling capital, adding RPT “cannot do the entire” multifamily opportunity alone and may need “a combination of third-party capital, Rithm Property Trust and Rithm.”

  • Jade Rahmani, KBW: asked about REIT combinations; Title Nierenberg said, “We'll continue to look at M&A,” and contrasted RPT with “broken REITs,” stating, “This REIT is not broken. This balance sheet is crystal clean.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing on scale and urgency, with Coffey arguing “this is kind of a chicken or an egg thing” and Rahmani highlighting a sector “under pressure for several years,” repeatedly returning to consolidation and capital-raising feasibility.

  • Management’s tone was neutral to slightly positive, stressing patience and flexibility; Nierenberg repeatedly pointed to optionality (“we'll continue to remain patient,” “we'll continue to pay the dividend”) while describing active evaluation of multifamily and debt opportunities.

  • Versus last quarter, the tone shifted from a recap-and-earnings narrative to a more waiting-and-selectivity posture; the current call leaned on “uneventful” results and redeployment timing rather than laying out a defined recap path.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q4 2025 emphasized a recap plan and a “future state where the company is earning something between $1.60 and $1.70 per share”; Q1 2026 did not repeat that target and instead stressed being “patient” and potentially pursuing “M&A” or “buying back a little bit of stock.”

  • Q1 2026 highlighted rotating out of “levered AAA CMBS” to build liquidity for “higher-yielding assets,” whereas Q4 2025 focused more on identified Genesis multifamily loan pools and “no J-curve” around a recap.

  • Analysts remained focused on scale in both quarters, but Q1’s questions concentrated more on near-term capital deployment cadence, CMBS sales, and Paramount ramp timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nierenberg underscored macro and sentiment risk: “the market felt or the world feels horrible,” and flagged that “headline risk” could take time to play out, particularly for “earlier vintages in the private credit world.”

  • Management’s mitigation centered on liquidity and selectivity: selling assets “to create more capital,” monitoring markets, and pursuing “higher-yielding assets,” while keeping the dividend in place.

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed Q1 as a low-activity quarter marked by liquidity-building and portfolio positioning, with an emphasis on maintaining the dividend and keeping optionality for redeploying cash into higher-yielding debt, multifamily opportunities expected to surface in May, and potential strategic actions including M&A or selective buybacks, while Paramount was described as flat in the quarter but expected to “ramp up” as execution progresses.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq rises, Dow lags as Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extension
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.2%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.5%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.3%.

Treasury yields were muted across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.34%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 1 basis point to 3.84%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington. 

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) stayed higher at $95, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $105 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Lionsgate's Michael Jackson biopic thrills audiences, beats box-office preview records
data.description:
Lionsgate's (LION) Michael has already earned more than $12.5M in previews, exceeding expectations and making the Michael Jackson film the best ever for a musical biopic and the studio’s top-earning movie since 2015’s Hunger Games: Mockingjay Part 2.

According to Deadline, Michael’s preview cash has already topped former box-office champ Project Hail Mary’s $12M and has earned $18.5M in international previews and opening-day receipts.

Directed by Antoine Fuqua and starring Michael Jackson’s nephew Jaafar Jackson, Michael chronicles the King of Pop’s life from his childhood in Gary, Indiana, to his meteoric rise with the Jackson 5 and global superstardom as an independent artist.

Focusing on his turbulent relationship with his father and creative process making the blockbuster Thriller album, the movie’s timeline ends in 1988 and, due to a legal settlement, avoids addressing accusations leveled against the pop star regarding inappropriate behavior involving children.

While the movie has received lackluster reviews from critics (40% Rotten Tomatoes critics score), particularly for omitting the most controversial years of the artist’s life, audiences have given the movie a 96% score, a Rotten Tomatoes record for a biopic.   

Michael opens in theaters and IMAX (IMAX) on Friday, April 24. Universal Pictures (CMCSA) is the film's international distributor.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: MXL, INTC, COUR, CHTR, MXL, and more
data.description:
Stock futures were mixed in the premarket  as investors weighed a complex geopolitical landscape against a backdrop of persistent energy risks.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • MaxLinear (MXL) +66% - Shares surged after delivering a strong Q1 beat and issuing well-above-consensus guidance, driven by a sharp inflection in its AI-linked optical data center business. The company guided Q2 revenue to $160M–$170M (vs. ~$144M estimates) and raised its 2026 optical data center outlook to $150M–$170M, citing accelerating demand for its Keystone interconnect products across hyperscale customers supporting 400G/800G AI workloads. While supply constraints and higher input costs persist, the upbeat outlook and visibility into AI infrastructure demand fueled the rally.
  • Intel (INTC) +25% - Shares jumped after delivering a strong Q1 beat and upbeat guidance, with adjusted EPS of $0.29 and revenue of $13.58B, both comfortably above expectations. Growth was driven by strength in Client Computing ($7.73B) and Data Center & AI ($5.05B, +22% Y/Y), while the company also pointed to sustained momentum with a sixth straight revenue beat. Looking ahead, Intel guided Q2 revenue to $13.8B–$14.8B and EPS to $0.20, both well ahead of consensus, as management highlighted rising demand from the shift toward AI inference and edge computing.
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +13% - Shares soared following stronger-than-expected results from Intel (INTC) and after D.A. Davidson & Co. upgraded the stock to Buy and sharply raised its price target, citing a “structural increase” in CPU demand driven by agentic AI. The call was reinforced by strong results from Intel Corporation, which analysts view as a positive read-through for AMD’s upcoming earnings, with expectations for upside revisions. The firm highlighted that rising demand for server CPUs in AI workloads could create supply tightness, positioning AMD to raise prices and expand margins, while benefiting from the broader data center buildout.
  • SAP SE (SAP) +7% - Shares gained after reporting Q1 results highlighted by strong cloud momentum, with cloud revenue jumping 27% (constant currency) and backlog surging 25% to $25.6B. While total revenue of $11.17B came in line with estimates, robust cloud growth and visibility supported outlook confidence, with the company guiding FY cloud revenue to $30.1B–$30.6B and combined cloud/software revenue growth of 12%–13%.
  • Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) +4% - Shares rose after JPMorgan Chase & Co. initiated coverage with an Overweight rating, citing improving fundamentals and multiple growth catalysts. The firm highlighted a stabilizing GLP-1 business, the recent partnership with Novo Nordisk reducing legal overhang, and potential upside from peptide legalization, where HIMS could benefit as an early mover. JPMorgan also sees revenue reacceleration in the second half and further branded partnerships supporting growth, setting a $35 price target (~24% upside).

Biggest stock losers

  • Coursera (COUR) -15% - Shares fell sharply after a wider-than-expected GAAP loss and declining free cash flow overshadowed otherwise solid operating metrics, including double-digit consumer revenue growth and a record 7.6M new learners in Q1. The company posted a net loss of $20.5M, nearly triple Y/Y, while maintaining full-year revenue guidance of $805M–$815M (in line with estimates) and projecting Q2 revenue slightly below consensus. Despite strong user growth and a stable long-term outlook, profitability concerns and softer near-term guidance weighed on sentiment.
  • Charter Communications (CHTR) -15% - Shares declined after mixed Q1 results, as a larger-than-expected broadband subscriber loss overshadowed modest strength elsewhere. The company lost 120K internet customers (vs. -100K expected), while mobile additions and better-than-feared video losses failed to offset weakness across core services. Revenue declined 1.1% to $13.5B (miss), EPS of $9.17 also missed, and EBITDA fell 2.2%, with free cash flow down 12.5%, reinforcing concerns around competitive pressure and slowing growth despite management’s longer-term improvement outlook.
  • HCA Healthcare (HCA) -7% - Shares tumbled after Q1 results showed softer-than-expected profitability and a lack of typical seasonal volume uplift, despite a modest revenue beat. While revenue rose ~4% Y/Y to $19.1B, adjusted EBITDA missed estimates, and margins compressed ~50 bps, reflecting weaker patient volumes driven by a sharp drop in respiratory cases and weather disruptions. The company also reiterated full-year guidance that remains below consensus, reinforcing concerns around volume trends and near-term earnings momentum.
  • Boyd Gaming (BYD) -6% - Shares slid after the company reported Q1 results that missed expectations, with non-GAAP EPS of $1.60 (-$0.11 miss) and revenue of $997.4M (+0.6% Y/Y), slightly below estimates. While management reiterated full-year expectations for its Online ($30M–$35M EBITDAR) and Managed & Other ($110M–$114M EBITDAR) segments, investor focus remained on ongoing pressure in the Las Vegas Locals segment, where destination trends and Suncoast-related disruptions have been weighing on performance. CFO commentary suggested a ~$5M–$6M quarterly EBITDAR drag persisting into Q2, with pressures expected through Q3 before gradual improvement, though a full recovery and return to growth may not materialize until early next year.
  • Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) -3% - Shares dipped after data showed its weight-loss drugs lagging rival uptake, with prescriptions for Zepbound declining ~2% week-over-week while Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy grew 7%. Early launch data for Lilly’s oral therapy Foundayo also came in soft, with ~3.7K scripts in its first full week versus ~18.4K for Novo’s comparable launch. While analysts cautioned against overinterpreting early data, the slower prescription trends weighed on sentiment as investors reassessed Lilly’s positioning in the obesity market.
